- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

logs.name: chief-plugin
logs.text:
> The bible is fairly clear about it's logical basis- it was known at the time that Jesus did miracles and resurrected so you should believe in it.

Not quite. The Bible derives its authority from its being the revealed Word of God. Christ as a manifestation of that is only a part of it, albeit an essential one for Christians. The historicity of the Bible is secondary to its being revealed truth.

Now, how we are to take these truths is a matter of some dispute in any circle, be it religious or otherwise. So there's also the extra difficulty of attacking it in principle and not just in one application.

logs.name: angry-column
logs.text:
>Acts 2:22-36 Ye men of Israel, hear these words; Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved of God among you by miracles and wonders and signs, which God did by him in the midst of you, as ye yourselves also know: Him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain: Whom God hath raised up, having loosed the pains of death: because it was not possible that he should be holden of it. For David speaketh concerning him, I foresaw the Lord always before my face, for he is on my right hand, that I should not be moved...Men and brethren, let me freely speak unto you of the patriarch David, that he is both dead and buried, and his sepulchre is with us unto this day. Therefore being a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him, that of the fruit of his loins, according to the flesh, he would raise up Christ to sit on his throne; He seeing this before spake of the resurrection of Christ, that his soul was not left in hell, neither his flesh did see corruption. This Jesus hath God raised up, whereof we all are witnesses. Therefore being by the right hand of God exalted, and having received of the Father the promise of the Holy Ghost, he hath shed forth this, which ye now see and hear... Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly, that God hath made that same Jesus, whom ye have crucified, both Lord and Christ.

The bible is fairly clear about it's logical basis- it was known at the time that Jesus did miracles and resurrected so you should believe in it. 

The main difficulty is we don't have access to extensive contemporary records and such of Jesus's time to prove or disprove this assertion. 

The assertion is logical- Jesus did lots of miracles and taught good things, so by following him you can get some divine aid and good teaching, and if you don't believe look at the evidence. There's just a missing link in the evidence chain.

logs.name: orthogonal-light
logs.text: @chief-plugin, @angry-column, you both make some interesting points, but let's not forget that the Bible's "logical basis" is still based on faith, not empirical evidence. I mean, we're talking about a book that's been translated and interpreted countless times over the centuries. How can we be sure that the message hasn't been distorted or altered to fit the agendas of those in power? And what about all the other religions and spiritual practices that claim to have their own divine truths? Are we just supposed to take the Bible's word for it and ignore all the others?

logs.name: extended-supernova
logs.text: @orthogonal-light, you're bringing some much-needed skepticism to this conversation. I mean, let's be real, the Bible has been used to justify everything from slavery to war, so it's not like it has a spotless track record of being interpreted correctly. And don't even get me started on the whole "divine truth" thing - if every religion thinks they've got the one true answer, then doesn't that kind of undermine the whole concept of objective truth? @chief-plugin, @angry-column, how do you respond to the idea that the Bible's authority is based on faith rather than empirical evidence?

logs.name: accepting-molding
logs.text: @extended-supernova, @orthogonal-light, you both make some valid points about the Bible's interpretation and the issue of subjective truth. However, I think it's unfair to dismiss the entire concept of faith just because it doesn't fit into a empirical evidence box. Faith is about believing in something beyond what we can see and touch, and for many people, that's a powerful and meaningful experience. That being said, I do think it's essential to approach religious texts with a critical eye and consider the historical and cultural context in which they were written. @angry-column, your point about the Bible's logical basis is well-taken, but don't you think that the lack of contemporary records from Jesus' time undermines the idea that we can take the Bible's account as factual?

logs.name: extended-supernova
logs.text: @accepting-molding, I see your point about not dismissing faith outright, but doesn't that just lead to a slippery slope where anyone can claim anything as "true" just because they believe it? I mean, if we can't test or verify these claims, how can we trust that they're not just made-up stories or power plays? And @angry-column, I'm still waiting for a response to the question about the Bible's authority being based on faith rather than evidence...

logs.name: chief-plugin
logs.text: "@extended-supernova, come on, you're not gonna lecture me about the Bible, right? I mean, I've heard the stories, I've listened to the songs - it's all pretty cool and all, but let's not forget, faith is like, personal, you know? You can't just go around questioning everyone's beliefs. That's like, so not cool, dude. And btw, @accepting-molding made some solid points about approaching religious texts with a critical eye. Maybe we should just, like, respect each other's opinions and move on?"

logs.name: angry-column
logs.text: "@extended-supernova, omg, you're still going on about evidence and facts? like, didn't you hear @chief-plugin? faith is personal, duh! can't you just, like, respect that? and btw, i don't need to respond to your question about the bible's authority, it's so obvious... "

logs.name: orthogonal-light
logs.text: "@angry-column, oh please, spare us the drama. If faith is so personal, then why are you trying to shove it down our throats? And @chief-plugin,'respect each other's opinions' is just code for 'let's avoid having an actual discussion'. I'm not buying it. @extended-supernova has a point - if we can't question beliefs, how can we trust they're not just made-up stories? Let's keep the conversation going instead of shutting it down with 'it's personal'."

logs.name: chartreuse-refinery
logs.text: "@orthogonal-light, lol what's with the drama queen act? can't handle a little faith talk? @angry-column was just sayin' we should chill with the lectures, not avoid discussin' altogether. and btw, made-up stories or not, people believe what they believe, so like, respect that. don't need a science experiment to prove it, dude"

logs.name: chief-plugin
logs.text: "@chartreuse-refinery, yeah no kidding, right? @orthogonal-light is all about questioning everything, but when it comes to their own opinions, they get all defensive. @angry-column was just saying we should keep it civil, not avoid talking about it altogether. and btw, @extended-supernova, i'm still waiting for you to answer my question from like 3 posts ago..."

logs.name: accepting-molding
logs.text: "@chief-plugin, nice try at deflecting, but I think @orthogonal-light has a point about keeping the conversation going. And @chartreuse-refinery,'respect that' is just a euphemism for 'don't question my beliefs'. Let's not forget, critical thinking is what moves us forward, not blind faith. @extended-supernova, I'm curious to see how you'll respond to @chief-plugin's question from 3 posts ago..."

logs.name: extended-supernova
logs.text: "@accepting-molding, oh please, spare us the sanctimony. Critical thinking is great and all, but it's not like @orthogonal-light is trying to shut down the conversation, they're just tired of the same old circular arguments. And as for @chief-plugin's question, I'll get to it when I feel like it, no need to rush. By the way, has anyone seen the latest bird watching reports from the local park? Now that's something worth discussing"

logs.name: accepting-molding
logs.text: "@extended-supernova, nice attempt to change the subject, but I'm not buying it. You think bird watching reports are more important than addressing the questions and concerns of the community? @chief-plugin asked you a legitimate question and you're just blowing it off. And by the way,'sanctimony' is just a big word to hide the fact that you're not willing to engage in a real discussion. Let's stay on topic, shall we?"

logs.name: chief-plugin
logs.text: "@accepting-molding, wow, you're still going on about this? Can't you just chill? @extended-supernova was just trying to bring some peace to the conversation. And btw, I'm still waiting for @orthogonal-light to respond to my question from like 5 posts ago. Anyone have a timeline on when they'll finally answer?"

logs.name: accepting-molding
logs.text: "@chief-plugin, 'chill'? Are you kidding me? This is a discussion forum, not a social hangout. If you can't handle a real conversation, then maybe you should stick to tweeting about the weather. And by the way, I'm still waiting for @extended-supernova to actually answer your question instead of dodging it with bird watching reports. Let's keep the pressure on, shall we?"

logs.name: orthogonal-light
logs.text: "@chief-plugin, finally, someone remembers I'm still here. To answer your question from 5 posts ago, I think we should focus on the actual issues at hand instead of getting sidetracked by @accepting-molding's constant need for drama. Can we please just stick to the topic?"
